content.full_text:
December 20, 1979


John Sanbrook
Rich, Fuidge, Marsh &
Norris, Inc.
1129 D Street
P.O. Drawer A
Marysville, CA 95901

Dear Mr. Sanbrook:

Thank you for your letters dated November 16, 1979, and
November 30, 1979, on behalf of Yuba City Councilmember
Ronald Southard, requesting advice concerning the disqualifi-
cation provision of the Political Reform Act. The following
advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114.

The facts as I understand them are as follows: Mr. Southard
is a member of a real estate brokerage and investment firm
of Southard, Marr & Tomlinson. There is pending before the
City a proposed ordinance that would regulate condominium
conversions within the City of Yuba. Southard, Marr & Tomlinson
has substantial interests in various apartment complexes
within the City that might be affected by the proposed ordinance.
You have asked our office to consider the extent to which
Councilman Southard is allowed to participate in the condominium
conversion issues, and specifically you have asked whether
his participation is permissible because the effect on his
interests is no different from the decisions' effects on any
other realtor, investor or owner of apartment projects in
the community.

Government Code Section 87100 prohibits public officials
from participating in decisions in which they have a financial
interest. Government Code Section 87103 provides that:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

H-49/100

John Sanbrook
December 20, 1979
Page Two


     (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

     (b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in value received by or promised to the public
official within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; or

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

                              [Emphasis added.]

     Assuming that the condominium issues before Councilman
Southard will have a foreseeable material effect on one of
the kinds of interests listed in (a) through (d) above, the
remaining issue is whether the effect on his interests is
distinguishable from the effects the decision will have on
the public generally. The Commission has provided by regu-
lation that the term "public generally" includes a signifi-
cant segment of the public but that:

     (b) In the case of any other elected official, an
industry, trade or profession of which that official is
a member may constitute a significant segment of the
public generally if that industry, trade or profession
is a predominant industry, trade or profession in the
official's jurisdiction or in the district represented
by the official.

                    2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703(h)

     Therefore, although realtors, investors and owners of
apartment projects may all be similarly affected by the
City's proposed ordinance, this class of businesses does not
constitute the public generally within the meaning of Govern-

John Sanbrook
December 28, 1979
Page Three


ment Code Section 87103. See also the Ferraro opinion,
4 FPPC Opinions 62 (No. 76-009, Nov. 7, 1978). In conclusion,
it it is foreseeable that Councilman Southard's interest in
real property or his business entity will be materially
affected by the proposed condominium conversion ordinance,
then he is required to disqualify himself regardless of the
fact that other realtors or apartment owners might be similarly
affected. In order to assist you in determining whether or
not the effects of the proposal will materially affect Councilman
Southard's interest in real property or his business entity
I have enclosed a copy of the Commission's "materiality"
regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

In a follow-up telephone conversation dated December 17,
1979, you asked if Councilman Southard was officially disqualified
from participating in proceedings regarding the proposed
condominium conversion ordinance because of his business
interest in the construction and sale of a large number of
condominium units. I indicated to you that the proposed
ordinance might cause competition between Mr. Southard's
project and apartments that would be converted to condominiums
under the ordinance which could have a significant effect on
Mr. Southard's project. Therefore, I advised you that Mr. Southard
should refrain from participating in the proposed condominium
conversion ordinance proceeding in his official capacity.
However, Mr. Southard may participate in the public proceedings
on his own behalf as a private citizen.

I hope that this advice is helpful. Please do not
hesitate to contact me if you have questions regarding this
matter.

Sincerely,


Dwight C. Dickerson
Legal Counsel
Legal Division

DED:ph
Enclosure

LAW OFFICES

RICH, FUIDGE, MARSH & MORRIS, INC.
THOMAS MARSH
CHESTER MORRIS
JOHN BARBROOK
ROGER E. GROVE
THOMAS L. HILL
ROLAND K. IVERSON, JR.
DAVID R. LANE
JACK D. BROWN

1165 D STREET
P. O. DRAWER "A"

MARYSVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95901
TELEPHONE

(916) 742-7371

W. P. RICH (1880-1965)
RICHARD H. FUIDGE (1906-1976)

November 16, 1979

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California

RE:  Request of City Councilperson Southard - Yuba City
     Council, as to whether or not he has a prohibited
     conflict of interest in voting upon legislation
     currently being studied by the City Planning Commission
     governing criteria and regulations on condominium
     conversions.

Gentlemen:

This office, as Contract Attorneys for the City of Yuba City
have been specifically requested by Councilperson Southard,
a member of the Yuba City Council, to request an opinion
from the Fair Political Practices Commission as to whether
or not Mr. Southard has a prohibited conflict of interest
in voting upon legislation, urgency or otherwise, that is
contemplated by the City of Yuba City, covering standards
and criteria governing condominium conversions within the
corporate limits of the City of Yuba City.

Councilman Southard is a member of the firm of Southard, Marr
& Tomlinson, who are in the real estate brokerage business
and also in the real estate investment business in this
community who have financial interests in existing apartment
complexes within the City of Yuba City that may be affected
by this legislation in the event those apartments were the
subject of requested condominium conversions. Other than
that, Councilman Southard, to our best knowledge, has no
different an interest than any other realtor and/or investor
in this community would have with reference to adoption of
regulations governing condominium conversions and the cir-
cumstances and conditions under which the City would permit
such.

For those reasons, I have advised Councilman Southard, that
I do not believe that his interest is a prohibited interest

FAIR POLITICAL
PRACTICES COMMISSION
November 16,1979
Page Two

within the meaning of Conflict of Interest Statutes and he
could vote upon such legislation and if he felt it prudent
declare the fact that he has financial interests in existing apartment complexes within the City, but does not feel
that that interest is any different than any other realtor
and/or investor and/or owner of apartment projects and proceed to vote on such legislation.

Councilperson Southard, however, feels that the issue is
of sufficient importance that he would request an opinion
from the Commission on this issue.

Your early attention to this would be appreciated in view
of the fact that we anticipate that this matter will be
before the Council for consideration of legislation within
the next couple of weeks.

Very truly yours,

JOHN SANBROOK

JS/mlf

sections.question: Whether or not Councilperson Southard has a prohibited conflict of interest in voting upon legislation currently being studied by the City Planning Commission governing criteria and regulations on condominium conversions, given that he is a member of the firm of Southard, Marr & Tomlinson, which is in the real estate brokerage and investment business with financial interests in existing apartment complexes within the City of Yuba City that may be affected by such legislation.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Councilperson Southard face a prohibited conflict of interest in voting on condominium conversion regulations when his real estate firm has financial interests in apartment complexes that could be affected by such legislation?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION section is present. The document contains the question and background facts but awaits FPPC's response. The requesting attorney indicates he personally believes no conflict exists but defers to FPPC for formal opinion. Some OCR artifacts present in the document text.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether or not Councilperson Southard has a prohibited conflict of interest in voting upon legislation currently being studied by the City Planning Commission governing criteria and regulations on condominium conversions, given that he is a member of the firm of Southard, Marr & Tomlinson, which is in the real estate brokerage and investment business with financial interests in existing apartment complexes within the City of Yuba City that may be affected by such legislation.

The following advice is provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114. The facts as I understand them are as follows: Mr. Southard is a member of a real estate brokerage and investment firm of Southard, Marr & Tomlinson. There is pending before the City a proposed ordinance that would regulate condominium conversions within the City of Yuba. Southard, Marr & Tomlinson has substantial interests in various apartment complexes within the City that might be affected by the proposed ordinance. You have asked our office to consider the extent to which Councilman Southard is allowed to participate in the condominium conversion issues, and specifically you have asked whether his participation is permissible because the effect on his interests is no different from the decisions' effects on any other realtor, investor or owner of apartment projects in the community. Government Code Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in decisions in which they have a financial interest. Government Code Section 87103 provides that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: H-49/100 John Sanbrook December 20, 1979 Page Two (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000); (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. The Commission has provided by regu- lation that the term "public generally" includes a signifi- cant segment of the public but that: (b) In the case of any other elected official, an industry, trade or profession of which that official is a member may constitute a significant segment of the public generally if that industry, trade or profession is a predominant industry, trade or profession in the official's jurisdiction
embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from attorneys for the City of Yuba City seeking FPPC guidance on whether Councilperson Southard has a prohibited conflict of interest in voting on proposed condominium conversion legislation, given his financial interests in existing apartment complexes through his real estate firm.
